summary: Gruber traces the evolution of Apple's relationship with IBM, from the iconic 1984 Super Bowl ad that cast IBM as Big Brother to the 2003 PowerMac G5 partnership where IBM supplies Apple's processors. He argues that Apple's polarizing brand identity — the 'us vs. them' marketing — invited strong opinions from non-users and created lasting perceptions of Apple as a corporate loner. The piece examines how the real enemy was always Microsoft, which undermined both Apple and IBM, while Motorola's decline as a semiconductor company left Apple needing a stronger hardware partner. Gruber concludes with the ironic observation that the new G5 Macs are literally more 'IBM-compatible' than any Dell or HP.
key_points: Apple uniquely attracts strong opinions from people who have never used its products, partly due to its culturally polarizing brand identity
The 1984 ad's Big Brother was IBM, not Microsoft — Microsoft was just one of several software companies at the time
Apple and IBM were never true enemies but opposites; their real common enemy was Microsoft, which deceived both companies
Motorola's decline from semiconductor leader to second-rate phone maker left Apple needing a credible hardware partner
The IBM-Apple G5 partnership gives Apple both a superior processor and the industry credibility that comes with IBM's name
money_quotes: One of the biggest differences between Apple and the vast majority of tech companies is that everyone tends to have an opinion about them.
Part of this is just foolishness, a by-product of the ignorant notion that everyone is entitled to an opinion on anything.
If there's an 'us', there must be a 'them'.
The ad said nothing about what the Macintosh was. It didn't even show a Macintosh. It served only to establish sides.
IBM and Apple circa 1984 weren't so much enemies as they were opposites.
Microsoft, which bamboozled the Sculley regime at Apple into inadvertently granting them carte blanche to blatantly steal elements of the Macintosh look and feel.
With the G5, Apple is getting much more than a terrific new processor. They've also gained a powerful and respected ally.
The new PowerMac G5 is, in a literal sense, more 'IBM-compatible' than a Wintel PC from Dell or HP.
key_insight: Apple's journey from casting IBM as Big Brother in 1984 to partnering with them on the G5 in 2003 illustrates how the tech industry's alliances are shaped not by ideology but by the pragmatic need for credible, powerful partners.
